questions.text: Which is lighter than dry air
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Moist air contains water vapor, which is lighter than dry air. Water vapor has a molar mass of approximately 18 g/mol, while the average molar mass of dry air is about 29 g/mol. Therefore, moist air is lighter than dry air.

SO2 (Sulfur Dioxide) - Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is a gas that is denser than dry air. It has a molar mass of about 64 g/mol, which is significantly heavier than the average molar mass of dry air (29 g/mol). Therefore, SO2 is heavier than dry air

CI2 (Chlorine) - Chlorine (CI2) is a gas that is also denser than dry air. It has a molar mass of about 71 g/mol, which is heavier than the average molar mass of dry air (29 g/mol). Consequently, CI2 is heavier than dry air.
O2 (Oxygen) - Oxygen (O2) is one of the main components of dry air. It has a molar mass of about 32 g/mol, which is slightly heavier than the average molar mass of dry air (29 g/mol).

questions.explanation: Air is a mixture of various gases, with nitrogen (N2) and oxygen (O2) being the main components. Dry air refers to the composition of air without any moisture or water vapor. In this context, we are comparing the relative densities of different substances with dry air.

questions.text: Which of the following is a physical property of gases?

questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: There are six primary properties of gases: expansion, fluidity, low density, compressibility, diffusion, and effusion. This alone is not correct.

There are six primary properties of gases: expansion, fluidity, low density, compressibility, diffusion, and effusion. This alone is not correct.

There are six primary properties of gases: expansion, fluidity, low density, compressibility, diffusion, and effusion. Expansion suggests that gases have no defined shape and will expand to fill a given space, without significant intermolecular interaction. Fluidity is a property of both gases and liquids and describes the relatively low attraction between particles. This allows the gas molecules to move past one another, creating the "fluid" nature of the gas. Low density of gases is linked to gas expansion. Gases will expand to the greatest extent possible, resulting in low mass per unit volume ratios. Compressibility is also linked to expansion and the indefinite shape of the gas, essentially suggesting that the distance between particles can be reduced if pressure is increased. Diffusion and effusion are both linked to the movement of gases. Diffusion means that gases can spread out and mix within a given space, while effusion means that gases can pass through a small opening at a given rate. Some of these properties are unique to gases, while others are shared between gases and liquids. Gases have virtually no physical properties in common with solids.

questions.text: The density of an ideal gas falls as its temperature and pressure?

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.The density of an ideal gas decreases as its temperature increases and its pressure decreases. This is because the density of a gas is proportional to its mass and inversely proportional to its volume. When the temperature of a gas increases, the molecules of the gas move faster and spread out, which increases the volume of the gas. When the pressure of a gas decreases, the volume of the gas increases. Therefore, both an increase in temperature and a decrease in pressure will cause the density of a gas to decrease.

Therefore, both an increase in temperature and a decrease in pressure will cause the density of a gas to decrease.The following equation shows the relationship between the density of an ideal gas, its temperature, and its pressure: Density = PM/RT where: P is the pressure of the gas M is the molar mass of the gas R is the ideal gas constant T is the temperature of the gas As you can see from the equation, the density of a gas is inversely proportional to its temperature and directly proportional to its pressure. Therefore, an increase in temperature or a decrease in pressure will cause the density of a gas to decrease.

There are two main assumptions for an ideal gas (and a few smaller assumptions). First, the gas particles of the ideal gas must have no molecular volume. Second, the gas particles must exert no intermolecular forces on each other; therefore, forces such hydrogen bonding, dipole-dipole interactions, and London dispersion forces are irrelevant in ideal gases. Other small assumptions of ideal gases include random particle motion (no currents), lack of intermolecular interaction with the container walls, and completely elastic collisions (a corollary of zero intermolecular forces). For real gases, however, these assumptions are invalid. This means that the real gas particles have molecular volume and exert intermolecular forces on each other. Recall that the volume in the ideal gas law is the volume of the free space available inside the container. For ideal gases, the free space volume is equal to the volume of the container because the gas particles take up no volume; however, for real gases, the free space volume is the volume of the container minus the volume of the gas particles. Though the exact values of free space volume will differ, the volume of the container is important for both real and ideal gases.

questions.text: A container with a pin-hole contains equal moles of H2(g) and O2(g). Find the fraction of oxygen gas escaped at the same time when one-fourth of hydrogen gas escape

questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option does not seem to accurately represent the fraction of oxygen that has escaped. It is not consistent with the stoichiometry of the reaction between hydrogen and oxygen.
This option also does not accurately represent the fraction of oxygen that has escaped. It does not take into account the stoichiometric ratio between hydrogen and oxygen.

This option is the correct answer. As explained earlier, when one-fourth of the hydrogen gas escapes, 1/8 mole of oxygen reacts, leaving 7/8 of the oxygen remaining. The fraction of oxygen that has escaped is then 1/8, which is the half of 1/4 (the fraction of hydrogen that has escaped).
This option represents the fraction of oxygen that has reacted with the escaping hydrogen, but it does not represent the fraction of oxygen that has escaped overall.

questions.explanation: To solve this question, we use the stoichiometry of the reaction between hydrogen and oxygen. The balanced equation for the reaction is: 2H2(g) + O2(g) →2H2O(g) This equation tells us that 1 mole of O2 reacts with 2 moles of H2 to produce 2 moles of H2O. Therefore, for the reaction to occur, we need twice as much hydrogen as oxygen. Given that one-fourth of the hydrogen gas has escaped, we know that 1/4 of the original amount of H2 has reacted. According to the stoichiometry, 1 mole of O2 reacts with 2 moles of H2. So, 1/4 mole of H2 would require 1/8 mole of O2 to react completely. Thus, the fraction of oxygen that has reacted is 1/8 of the original amount. Consequently, the fraction of oxygen that has not reacted is 1 - 1/8 = 7/8. Since the question asks for the fraction of oxygen that has escaped at the same time when one-fourth of the hydrogen gas has escaped, we find the ratio of the fraction of oxygen that has escaped to the fraction of hydrogen that has escaped, which is 1/8 ÷ 1/4 = 1/2. Hence, the correct answer is 1/2, as the fraction of oxygen that has escaped at the same time when one-fourth of the hydrogen gas has escaped is half of the fraction of hydrogen that has escaped.

questions.text: Which inert gas is mixed with oxygen gas by deep-sea divers to adjust the partial pressure of oxygen gas?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: False!A mix of helium and oxygen is often used by deep sea divers for breathing because it reduces the risk of developing decompression sickness, also known as "the bends." The bends can occur when a diver ascends too quickly from a deep dive, causing nitrogen bubbles to form in their bloodstream and tissues. These bubbles can cause joint pain, fatigue, and even neurological damage in severe cases. Helium, which has a lower molecular weight than nitrogen, is less soluble in blood and tissues and therefore reduces the risk of bubble formation during ascent. Oxygen, on the other hand, is essential for sustaining life and provides the necessary gas for cellular respiration. However, high levels of oxygen can be toxic to the body and can cause seizures and other adverse effects. Therefore, a mixture of helium and oxygen is used to balance the benefits of reduced nitrogen exposure with the need for a sufficient oxygen supply.

The ideal gas law can be used to solve this problem. The equation is: PV = nRT Where: P = pressure (in atm) V = volume (in liters) n = number of moles R = ideal gas constant (0.0821 L.atm/mol.K) T = temperature (in Kelvin)First, we need to find the number of moles of hydrogen gas in the cylinder. We can use the following equation:PV = nRTn = PV/RTn = (5 dm3 x 10atm) / (0.0821 L.atm/mol.K x 293 K)n = 202.5 molesNext, we need to find the number of moles of hydrogen gas required to fill one balloon:n = PV/RTn = (0.25 dm3 x 1 atm) / (0.0821 L.atm/mol.K x 293 K)n = 0.0100 molesFinally, we can divide the total number of moles of hydrogen gas in the cylinder by the number of moles required for each balloon:202.5 moles / 0.0100 moles per balloon = 2025 balloonsTherefore, the answer is 200, which is the closest option.

Speed and distance are linear measures, frequency is a measure of cycles per time but not change rate, and gravity is a force, not a measure of change in motion over time.

questions.text: Work done by the centripetal force on a body moving in circle is zero because?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The body moves parallel to F
options.explanation: If the body moves parallel to the centripetal force (F), then the work done by the centripetal force will be zero. However, this is not the reason why the work done by the centripetal force on a body moving in a circle is zero. In a circular motion, the centripetal force is always directed toward the center of the circle, and the body moves at right angles to this force.

options.text: The body moves opposite to F
options.explanation: If the body moves opposite to the centripetal force, then the work done by the centripetal force would be negative. However, this is not the case for circular motion. The body always moves in the direction of the centripetal force, toward the center of the circle.

options.text: The body moves at right angles to F
options.explanation: This option correctly explains why the work done by the centripetal force is zero. In circular motion, the centripetal force is always directed toward the center of the circle, while the body moves at right angles to this force. Since the force and the displacement are perpendicular to each other, the work done is zero.

options.text: The centripetal and centrifugal forces balance each other
options.explanation: The concept of "centrifugal force" is often used in non-inertial reference frames but is not an actual force. The apparent "outward" force experienced in a rotating reference frame is due to the inertia of the object trying to move in a straight line tangent to the circle. The centripetal force is the real force that keeps the body moving in a circle. There is no balance between centripetal and centrifugal forces; rather, the centripetal force is responsible for keeping the body in circular motion.

questions.text: A solid disc, a ring, a solid sphere and a solid wheel having the same mass and radius are released from rest at the top of an inclined plane. Assuming that the objects roll down the plane without slipping, which of the following objects will reach the bottom of the plane first?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Based on the rotational inertia and mass distribution, the solid sphere will reach the bottom of the inclined plane first, followed by the solid disc, the solid wheel, and finally the ring. Hence, option D is the correct answer.

Based on the rotational inertia and mass distribution, the solid sphere will reach the bottom of the inclined plane first, followed by the solid disc, the solid wheel, and finally the ring. Hence, option D is the correct answer.

Based on the rotational inertia and mass distribution, the solid sphere will reach the bottom of the inclined plane first, followed by the solid disc, the solid wheel, and finally the ring. Hence, option D is the correct answer.

When objects roll down an inclined plane without slipping, their motion is determined by their rotational inertia and their distribution of mass. A solid sphere has the smallest rotational inertia among the given objects. The rotational inertia of a solid sphere is given by I = (2/5) * m * r^2, where m is the mass and r is the radius of the sphere. The mass is evenly distributed in a solid sphere, which results in a aller rotational inertia. This allows the solid sphere to accelerate more quickly down the inclined plane compared to the other objects. On the other hand, a solid disc, a ring, and a solid wheel all have larger rotational inertia. The moment of inertia of a solid disc is given by I = (1/2) * m * r^2, and the moment of inertia of a ring is given by I = m * r^2. These objects have more mass concentrated towards the outer edges, which increases their rotational inertia and slows their descent down the incline. The solid wheel, which is similar to a disc but with spokes or open regions, also has a larger rotational inertia. Therefore, based on the rotational inertia and mass distribution, the solid sphere will reach the bottom of the inclined plane first, followed by the solid disc, the solid wheel, and finally the ring. Hence, option D is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: When objects roll down an inclined plane without slipping, their speed is affected by their rotational inertia. The object with the smallest rotational inertia will accelerate fastest and reach the bottom first. The solid sphere, with its moment of inertia I = (2/5) * m * r^2, has the smallest rotational inertia due to its evenly distributed mass, allowing it to reach the bottom first. The solid disc, with I = (1/2) * m * r^2, follows the sphere as it has a slightly larger rotational inertia. The wheel, depending on its structure, has a larger rotational inertia than the disc but smaller than a ring, which has the largest moment of inertia I = m * r^2 due to its mass being concentrated at the edge. Therefore, the order from fastest to slowest is: solid sphere, solid disc, wheel, and ring.

questions.text: The mean angular velocity 'ω' of of the second hand of the given clock is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To calculate the mean angular velocity (ω) of the second hand of the clock, we need to determine the total angle covered by the second hand in a given time interval. In this case, the clock is striking 10:09, and the second hand is at 37 seconds. To find the total angle covered, we can use the formula: θ = (number of seconds) × (360 degrees / 60 seconds) θ = 37 seconds × (360 degrees / 60 seconds) θ = 222 degrees Now, we need to convert the angle to radians since the options are given in rad/sec. There are 2π radians in a full circle (360 degrees), so we can convert the angle to radians using the conversion factor: θ (in radians) = θ (in degrees) × (π radians / 180 degrees) θ (in radians) = 222 degrees × (π radians / 180 degrees) θ (in radians) ≈ 3.874 radians Next, we need to calculate the time interval. The given time is 37 seconds. The mean angular velocity is the angle covered divided by the time taken: ω = θ (in radians) / t (in seconds) ω = 3.874 radians / 37 seconds Calculating this value gives us approximately 0.105 rad/sec.

questions.text: Angular velocity cannot be

options.explanation: Angular velocity can be zero. This simply means that the object is not rotating around the axis. For example, if a spinning top comes to a complete stop, its angular velocity would be zero.

Angular velocity can be negative. A negative angular velocity indicates that the object is rotating in the opposite direction. For example, if a spinning top starts rotating clockwise (positive angular velocity) and then reverses its direction to rotate counterclockwise (negative angular velocity), the angular velocity would be negative.

Angular velocity cannot be infinite. An infinite angular velocity would mean that the object is rotating infinitely fast, which is not physically possible. In reality, there are limits to how fast an object can rotate due to factors such as material strength and external forces.

The option "Linear" is not directly related to angular velocity. Linear motion refers to motion in a straight line, without any rotation involved. Angular velocity specifically describes the rate of rotation around an axis. The two concepts are distinct, and angular velocity is not measured in linear units like meters or kilometers.
